era_context: The early 16th century: the Protestant Reformation is in its early stages under Martin Luther; the Spanish conquest of the Aztec Empire is reaching its climax; the Ottoman Empire under Süleyman the Magnificent is expanding aggressively; the Ming Dynasty is in its mid-reign period; and European exploration of the Americas is accelerating. This is a period of religious upheaval, imperial consolidation, and global exploration.

events.title: Martin Luther is excommunicated by Pope Leo X
events.description: Pope Leo X formally excommunicates Martin Luther on January 3, 1521, following Luther's refusal to recant his theological positions at the Diet of Worms in April 1521. The excommunication is the culmination of a conflict that began with Luther's posting of the 95 Theses in 1517. Luther's excommunication marks a decisive break between the Protestant reformers and the Roman Catholic Church and establishes Luther as the leader of a new religious movement. The excommunication also has political implications, as it involves the Holy Roman Emperor and various German princes who must decide whether to support Luther or the Pope.

sources.name: Papal bull of excommunication (Decet Romanum Pontificem)
Contemporary chronicles and records of the Diet of Worms

events.certainty_note: Luther's excommunication is one of the most well-documented events of the Reformation. The date and significance are firmly established.

events.title: Siege of Belgrade by Ottoman forces under Süleyman the Magnificent
events.description: Ottoman forces under Sultan Süleyman I besiege and capture the fortress city of Belgrade (Beograd) in August 1521. The siege lasts approximately two months, and the city falls to Ottoman forces after a fierce defense by Hungarian and Serbian troops. The capture of Belgrade is a major Ottoman victory and marks the beginning of Süleyman's expansion into Central Europe. The fall of Belgrade opens the way for Ottoman advances into Hungary and the Balkans, and it is followed by the Battle of Mohács in 1526, which will establish Ottoman dominance in the region.
events.certainty_note: The Siege of Belgrade is extensively documented in both Ottoman and European sources. The date and outcome are firmly established.

events.title: Fall of Tenochtitlan; conquest of the Aztec Empire by Spanish forces
events.description: After a three-month siege, the Aztec capital of Tenochtitlan falls to Spanish forces under Hernán Cortés on August 13, 1521. The siege is one of the most significant military events of the early modern period, resulting in the destruction of the Aztec Empire and the establishment of Spanish colonial rule in Mexico. The fall of Tenochtitlan is facilitated by Spanish military technology (firearms, horses), indigenous allies (particularly the Tlaxcalans), and the devastating impact of European diseases (particularly smallpox) on the indigenous population. The conquest results in the death of hundreds of thousands of indigenous people and the destruction of Aztec civilization. The fall of Tenochtitlan marks the beginning of Spanish colonial rule in Mexico and the transformation of the Americas.

sources.name: Cortés's letters to Charles V (primary accounts of the conquest)
Indigenous codices and later compilations (e.g., Florentine Codex by Sahagún)

events.certainty_note: The fall of Tenochtitlan is extensively documented in Spanish accounts and indigenous sources. The date of August 13, 1521, is firmly established.

events.title: Death of Ferdinand Magellan in the Philippines
events.description: Ferdinand Magellan, the Portuguese explorer leading an expedition to circumnavigate the globe, is killed in the Battle of Mactan on April 27, 1521, in the Philippines. Magellan is killed while fighting indigenous forces led by Chief Lapulapu. Although Magellan does not survive to complete the circumnavigation, his expedition continues under the command of Juan Sebastián Elcano and returns to Spain in 1522. Magellan's death marks the end of his personal involvement in the expedition, but the voyage continues and ultimately proves the feasibility of a westward route to Asia.
key_figures: Juan Sebastián Elcano (Spanish navigator, takes command)

sources.name: Antonio Pigafetta's account of the voyage (eyewitness chronicle)

events.certainty_note: Magellan's death is documented in Pigafetta's eyewitness account and Spanish records. The date of April 27, 1521, is firmly established.

events.title: Diet of Worms; Charles V confronts Martin Luther
events.description: The Diet of Worms, a formal assembly of the Holy Roman Empire, convenes in January 1521 to address the religious crisis posed by Martin Luther's Reformation. Luther is summoned to appear before the Diet and is asked to recant his theological positions. Luther refuses to recant, declaring that his conscience is bound by scripture and that he cannot be forced to abandon his beliefs. The Diet issues the Edict of Worms in May 1521, which declares Luther a heretic and an outlaw of the Empire. However, Luther is protected by Frederick the Wise, the Elector of Saxony, who shelters him in Wartburg Castle. The Diet of Worms marks a decisive moment in the Reformation, as it establishes Luther's defiance of both the Pope and the Emperor and demonstrates the political divisions within the Empire over religious reform.

sources.name: Official records of the Diet of Worms

events.certainty_note: The Diet of Worms is extensively documented in official records and contemporary accounts. The dates and significance are firmly established.

disconfirming_evidence: The narrative of Luther's excommunication as a decisive break between Protestantism and Catholicism has been complicated by modern scholarship, which emphasizes the gradual nature of the split and the continued efforts at reconciliation in the 1520s and 1530s. Additionally, the fall of Tenochtitlan is sometimes portrayed as a triumph of Spanish military superiority, but modern scholarship emphasizes the role of indigenous allies, disease, and indigenous political divisions in the conquest. The significance of Magellan's death is sometimes minimized, but modern scholarship recognizes that his death marked the end of his personal involvement in a voyage that was ultimately completed by others. The Diet of Worms is sometimes presented as a decisive confrontation, but modern scholarship emphasizes the political complexities and the continued negotiations between Luther, the Emperor, and various German princes.
historiographic_note: The documentation for 1521 is rich for European events, particularly the Reformation and the Diet of Worms, which are extensively recorded in contemporary sources. However, the perspective is heavily European and often focuses on elite political and religious figures. Indigenous perspectives on the fall of Tenochtitlan are preserved primarily in later compilations and codices, which may reflect post-conquest reinterpretations. The narrative of the Reformation emphasizes religious and intellectual history, potentially underrepresenting economic and social factors. The fall of Tenochtitlan is sometimes presented as a straightforward military victory, but modern scholarship emphasizes the complexity of the conquest and the agency of indigenous peoples.

graph_edges.note: Luther's excommunication was the culmination of the conflict that began with the 95 Theses
The Diet of Worms was a direct response to Luther's Reformation
Luther's excommunication and the Diet of Worms were part of the same religious crisis
The Siege of Belgrade opened the way for Ottoman expansion that culminated in the Battle of Mohács
The fall of Tenochtitlan completed the conquest that began with Cortés's arrival in 1519
Magellan's death led to the continuation of the expedition under Elcano, which returned in 1522
